per-capita-co-emissions-from-internation:
Per capita CO₂ emissions from international commercial passenger flights, tourism-adjusted

International aviation emissions are allocated to the country of departure, then adjusted for tourism.

This choropleth world map for 2024 uses a scale running from 0 kg through 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, and up to 1,000 kg per capita, with darker orange and red shades marking higher values, alongside a hatched pattern for no data. Canada stands out in the deepest red band at the top of the scale. The United Kingdom, Ireland, and a small cluster in Northern Europe also register in a dark red tone. The United States sits in a mid-to-dark orange shade, one band lighter than Canada. A deep red band also covers a country on the Arabian Peninsula, with Saudi Arabia prominently colored in dark orange-red. New Zealand is shown in a dark orange hue at the lower-right of the map. Much of continental Europe falls within mid-range orange shades, while most of Latin America, South and Southeast Asia, and the visible portions of East Asia sit in the lightest yellow-orange bands near the low end of the scale. Large areas of Africa, Central Asia, much of the Middle East outside the Gulf, Greenland, Australia, and scattered territories across the map are covered by diagonal hatching denoting missing data. The overall pattern places the highest per-capita values in North America, parts of Northern Europe, the Arabian Peninsula, and Oceania, with lower values across most of the rest of the mapped world.
banning-of-chick-culling:
Which countries have banned chick culling?

Chick culling is the process of separating and killing unwanted male and unhealthy female chicks that cannot produce eggs in industrialized egg facilities.

This choropleth world map, dated 2026, sorts countries into five categories shown in the legend: Banned (dark blue), Banned but not yet in effect (pale green), Not banned (brown), Partially banned (purple), and No data (hatched diagonal lines). A small cluster in Western and Central Europe is colored dark blue, with France and Germany among the blue-shaded countries. One country in this European cluster is shown in pale green, marking a ban that is not yet in effect. A purple shade is also visible within the same European cluster, corresponding to the partial-ban category. The note clarifies that in Switzerland grinding is banned while gassing is still allowed, and that Belgium has only a regional ban, placing these two in the partial category. Brown shading covers the United States, Canada, Mexico, most of South America, Australia, and a number of other individual countries scattered across Europe and elsewhere, marking them as not banned. The majority of the remaining land area, including most of Africa, most of Asia, Russia, and Greenland, is covered by diagonal hatching denoting no data. The visual pattern concentrates the banned and partial-ban categories within a compact part of Europe, while the rest of the mapped world is split between the not-banned category and large hatched regions without reported information.
child-deaths-from-invasive-non-typhoidal:
Invasive non-typhoidal salmonella deaths in children under five

Estimated annual number of deaths from invasive non-typhoidal salmonella in children under five.

This choropleth world map for 2023 uses a color scale running from pale cream near zero through yellow and orange to dark brown at 30,000, with thresholds at 10, 30, 100, 300, 1,000, 3,000, 10,000, and 30,000, plus a hatched pattern for no data. The darkest brown shading is concentrated in West Africa, with Nigeria standing out in a deep brown tone at the upper end of the scale. Surrounding countries across West, Central, and East Africa are filled with mid-to-dark orange hues, forming a broad band of elevated values that extends from the Atlantic coast across to the Horn of Africa and down through parts of Southern Africa. India is shown in a mid-orange shade, placing it among the higher bands in Asia. Pakistan and several neighboring South and Southeast Asian countries display lighter orange tones. Most of Europe, North America, South America, Australia, and the remainder of Asia are colored in the palest cream shades, corresponding to values near the low end of the scale. A small number of territories, including Greenland, are marked with diagonal hatching to denote missing data. The visual pattern places the heaviest estimated counts across Sub-Saharan Africa, with a secondary concentration in parts of South Asia, and very low values across most of the rest of the mapped world.
human-development-index-groups:
Human Development Index groups

The Human Development Index (HDI) is a summary measure of key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, a good education, and a decent standard of living. The groups below define different levels of human development depending on the HDI values.

This choropleth world map for 2023 assigns each country to one of four HDI groups: Low (red), Medium (yellow), High (light blue), or Very high (dark blue), with diagonal hatching for no data. Dark blue covers North America, most of Europe, Russia, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, South Korea, and Argentina and Chile in the southern cone of South America, as well as several Gulf states on the Arabian Peninsula. Light blue spans China, much of the rest of South America including Brazil, Mexico, parts of Southeast Asia, and several countries around the Mediterranean and in the Caucasus. Yellow fills India, Indonesia, and a wide belt across parts of North Africa, the Sahel, Southern Africa, and pockets of Southeast Asia. Red is concentrated in a band of Sub-Saharan African countries stretching across the continent, along with a country in the Horn of Africa region, Yemen on the Arabian Peninsula, and Afghanistan in South Asia. Greenland is covered by diagonal hatching marking no data. The overall pattern places the Very high group across most of the Northern Hemisphere's higher-income regions and Oceania, the High group across much of Latin America and parts of Eurasia, the Medium group through South and Southeast Asia and parts of Africa, and the Low group in a cluster of countries across central Africa and southwestern Asia.
ratio-of-same-day-to-tourist-trips:
Ratio of same-day trips to tourist trips

Same-day trips are those by visitors who arrive and depart within the same day. Tourist trips are those by people who stay overnight. A country with a ratio greater than one has more trips by visitors who leave the same day than those that stay overnight.

This choropleth world map for 2024 uses a diverging scale running from dark green at 0 through pale green at 0.5, then into pale and deeper purple shades at 1, 2, 3, and 4, with a hatched pattern for no data. Green shades dominate across much of South America, large parts of Sub-Saharan Africa, and several countries in Southeast Asia and Europe, marking ratios below 1 where overnight stays outnumber same-day visits. The United States is colored in a pale purple shade, placing it in the 1 to 2 range where same-day trips moderately exceed overnight stays. A cluster of countries in Central and Eastern Europe shows deeper purple tones in the 2 to 3 or higher bands. One country in the Horn of Africa region is also shown in a deep purple shade. Diagonal hatching covers extensive areas, including Canada, most of Russia and Central Asia, China, India, Australia, and many countries across the Middle East and parts of Africa, indicating that data is not available. The visual pattern places the highest same-day-to-overnight ratios in parts of Europe and North America, lower ratios across much of Latin America and Africa, and large hatched regions without reported values spanning northern Asia and Oceania.
uk-inflation-and-unemployment-rate-19892:
Inflation and unemployment rate in the UK

Unit 15 'Inflation, unemployment, and monetary policy' Section 15.7 'Supply shocks and inflation' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at: https://tinyco.re/15071330 [Figure 15.13]

This scatter-and-line chart plots the annual combination of inflation rate and unemployment rate for the United Kingdom from 1989 to 2020, with individual years labeled along a red connecting path. The horizontal axis shows the unemployment rate from 4% to 11%, and the vertical axis shows the inflation rate from 0% to 8%. A legend at the upper right identifies the series as Europe, with a small time-arrow running from 1989 to 2020. The trajectory begins in 1989 near 7% unemployment and roughly 5.5% inflation, then rises sharply upward to 1990 at about 8% inflation and similar unemployment. From 1990 the path sweeps down and to the right, reaching 1993 near 10.5% unemployment and around 2.5% inflation. The line then moves leftward through 1994, 1995, and 1996, dropping back toward 8% unemployment and inflation near 2 to 3%. Through the late 1990s and 2000s the points cluster between about 5% and 6% unemployment with inflation between roughly 0.5% and 3.5%, with 2000 sitting near the lower edge. Labels for 2008 and 2009 sit near 6% and 8% unemployment respectively, and 2011 appears at the upper end of this cluster near 4% inflation. The most recent labeled points, 2017 and 2018, sit at the far left between 4% and 5% unemployment with inflation around 2%.
grocery-bag-environmental-impact:
Environmental impacts of different types of grocery bags

Number of times a given grocery bag type would have to be reused to have an environmental impact as low as a standard single-use plastic bag.

This figure arranges eight small horizontal bar charts, one per bag type: Recycled PET, Biopolymer, Bleached paper, Conventional cotton, Organic cotton, Polyester PET recycled, Polypropylene woven recycled, and Unbleached paper. Each panel lists several indicators with a numeric count of reuses required to match a standard single-use plastic bag. Organic cotton records the largest values, with ozone depletion at 20,000 uses, water use at 3,830, freshwater eutrophication at 3,326, terrestrial acidification at 755, greenhouse gas emissions at 149, and an average across all indicators of 2,375. Conventional cotton follows with ozone depletion at 7,069, water use at 1,358, freshwater eutrophication at 1,178, terrestrial acidification at 265, greenhouse gas emissions at 52, and an average of 840. The remaining panels sit in the low tens to low hundreds. Recycled PET lists 96, 68.5, 44.7, 25, 12.6, and 8 uses across its indicators. Biopolymer shows 41.8, 9.7, 8, 7.8, 0, and -2.3. Bleached paper shows 43, 18.2, 13.6, 11, 5.9, and 1. Polyester PET recycled shows 35.1, 21.6, 14.6, 8, 3.6, and 2. Polypropylene woven recycled shows 31.9, 30, 25.9, 10, 5, and 4.8. Unbleached paper shows 43, 13.6, 9, 7.9, 3.6, and 0. A note explains that the average across all indicators incorporates additional metrics not shown, including marine eutrophication, particulate matter, and toxicity.
